author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
Pelagius Places Free Will at the Basis of All Turning to God for Grace.
title: Pelagius Places Free Will at the Basis of All Turning to God for Grace.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: But perhaps he may meet us with this rejoinder, that in the sentence before us he spoke of our “meriting the divine grace by doing the will of God,” in the sense that grace is added to those who believe and lead godly lives, whereby they may boldly withstand the tempter; whereas their very first reception of grace was, that they might do the will of God.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Lest, then, he make such a rejoinder, consider some other words of his on this subject: “The man,” says he, “who hastens to the Lord, and desires to be directed by Him, that is, who makes his own will depend upon God’s, who moreover cleaves so closely to the Lord as to become (as the apostle says) ‘one spirit’ with Him, does all this by nothing else than by his freedom of will.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Observe how great a result he has here stated to be accomplished only by our freedom of will; and how, in fact, he supposes us to cleave to God without the help of God: for such is the force of his words, “by nothing else than by his own freedom of will.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: So that, after we have cleaved to the Lord without His help, we even then, because of such adhesion of our own, deserve to be assisted.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: For he goes on to say: “Whosoever makes a right use of this” (that is, rightly uses his freedom of will), “does so entirely surrender himself to God, and does so completely mortify his own will, that he is able to say with the apostle, ‘Nevertheless it is already not I that live, but Christ liveth in me;’ and ‘He placeth his heart in the hand of God, so that He turneth it whithersoever He willeth.’”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Great indeed is the help of the grace of God, so that He turns our heart in whatever direction He pleases.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: But according to this writer’s foolish opinion, however great the help may be, we deserve it all at the moment when, without any assistance beyond the liberty of our will, we hasten to the Lord, desire His guidance and direction, suspend our own will entirely on His, and by close adherence to Him become one spirit with Him.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Now all these vast courses of goodness we (according to him) accomplish, forsooth, simply by the freedom of our own free will; and by reason of such antecedent merits we so secure His grace, that He turns our heart which way soever He pleases.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Well, now, how is that grace which is not gratuitously conferred?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: How can it be grace, if it is given in payment of a debt?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: How can that be true which the apostle says, “It is not of yourselves, but it is the gift of God; not of works, lest any man should boast;” and again, “If it is of grace, then is it no more of works, otherwise grace is no more grace:” how, I repeat, can this be true, if such meritorious works precede as to procure for us the bestowal of grace?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Surely, under the circumstances, there can be no gratuitous gift, but only the recompense of a due reward.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Is it the case, then, that in order to find their way to the help of God, men run to God without God’s help?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: And in order that we may receive God’s help while cleaving to Him, do we without His help cleave to God?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: What greater gift, or even what similar gift, could grace itself bestow upon any man, if he has already without grace been able to make himself one spirit with the Lord by no other power than that of his own free will?
